description: Punitive articles overview, Article 15/NJP, courts-martial types, rights of the accused, Article 31 rights, commander authority, fraternization, AWOL vs desertion, maximum punishments. Based on the Manual for Courts-Martial.

questions.q: What does UCMJ stand for?
questions.a: Uniform Code of Military Justice.

questions.q: When was the UCMJ enacted?
questions.a: May 5, 1950. It became effective on May 31, 1951.

questions.q: What is the Manual for Courts-Martial (MCM)?
questions.a: The comprehensive guide that contains the UCMJ, Rules for Courts-Martial, Military Rules of Evidence, and maximum punishments. It is an Executive Order issued by the President.

questions.q: Who is subject to the UCMJ?
questions.a: All members of the uniformed services (active duty, reserves on active duty, cadets/midshipmen, retirees receiving pay, POWs, and certain civilians accompanying the force in the field).

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers nonjudicial punishment (NJP)?
questions.a: Article 15.

questions.q: What is the common name for Article 15 in each service?
questions.a: Army/Air Force: Article 15. Navy/Marines: Captain's Mast or Admiral's Mast. Coast Guard: Captain's Mast.

questions.q: What is the purpose of nonjudicial punishment under Article 15?
questions.a: To allow commanders to resolve minor offenses quickly without the stigma or formality of a court-martial, while maintaining good order and discipline.

questions.q: Can a service member refuse Article 15 and demand a trial by court-martial?
questions.a: Yes, unless the member is attached to or embarked on a vessel (Navy/Marines), in which case the right to refuse is not available.

questions.q: What are the three types of Article 15 based on the imposing commander's rank?
questions.a: Summarized Article 15, Company-grade Article 15, and Field-grade Article 15.

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment a company-grade commander (O-3 and below) can impose under Article 15?
questions.a: 14 days extra duty, 14 days restriction, reduction of one grade (E-4 and below), forfeiture of 7 days base pay.

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment a field-grade commander (O-4 and above) can impose under Article 15?
questions.a: 45 days extra duty, 60 days restriction, reduction of one or more grades (E-4 and below; one grade for E-5/E-6), forfeiture of half a month's pay for 2 months.

questions.q: Can a service member appeal an Article 15?
questions.a: Yes. The member has the right to appeal to the next superior authority within 5 calendar days (or a reasonable time as specified).

questions.q: What are the three types of courts-martial?
questions.a: Summary Court-Martial, Special Court-Martial, and General Court-Martial.

questions.q: Who can convene a Summary Court-Martial?
questions.a: A commanding officer, typically at the battalion/squadron level or above, as specified in Article 24 of the UCMJ.

questions.q: How many members sit on a Summary Court-Martial?
questions.a: One commissioned officer.

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment a Summary Court-Martial can impose?
questions.a: 30 days confinement, 45 days hard labor without confinement, restriction for 2 months, forfeiture of two-thirds pay for one month, reduction to E-1.

questions.q: Can an accused refuse a Summary Court-Martial?
questions.a: Yes. The accused must consent to trial by Summary Court-Martial (it cannot be imposed without consent, unless the accused is absent without authority).

questions.q: Who can convene a Special Court-Martial?
questions.a: A commander empowered by the service regulations, typically at the brigade/wing level or any commander specifically authorized (Article 23).

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment a Special Court-Martial can impose?
questions.a: 12 months confinement, forfeiture of two-thirds pay per month for 12 months, reduction to E-1, a bad-conduct discharge (if a military judge is detailed and a verbatim record is made).

questions.q: Who can convene a General Court-Martial?
questions.a: The President, Secretary of Defense, Secretary of a military department, or a commanding general/flag officer as designated (Article 22).

questions.q: What is required before a General Court-Martial can be convened?
questions.a: A thorough investigation under Article 32 of the UCMJ (preliminary hearing).

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment a General Court-Martial can impose?
questions.a: Any punishment authorized by law for the offense, including death (for certain offenses), dishonorable discharge, total forfeiture of pay and allowances, and confinement for life.

questions.q: What does Article 31 of the UCMJ address?
questions.a: The right against compulsory self-incrimination (similar to the Fifth Amendment and Miranda rights in civilian law).

questions.q: What are the three key rights under Article 31?
questions.a: 1) Right to be informed of the nature of the accusation, 2) Right to be advised that any statement may be used against them, 3) Right to remain silent (no adverse inference from silence).

questions.q: How does Article 31 differ from civilian Miranda rights?
questions.a: Article 31 applies whenever a person subject to the UCMJ questions a suspect, not just during custodial interrogation. It has a broader application than Miranda.

questions.q: Does an accused service member have the right to an attorney at a court-martial?
questions.a: Yes. The accused has the right to a military defense counsel free of charge at Special and General Courts-Martial, and the right to hire a civilian attorney at their own expense.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers AWOL (Absence Without Leave)?
questions.a: Article 86 — Absence Without Leave.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers Desertion?
questions.a: Article 85 — Desertion.

questions.q: What is the key difference between AWOL and desertion?
questions.a: AWOL is unauthorized absence. Desertion requires the intent to remain away permanently or to avoid hazardous duty or shirk important service. Absence over 30 days raises a presumption of desertion.

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment for desertion during wartime?
questions.a: Death, or such other punishment as a court-martial may direct.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers disrespect toward a superior commissioned officer?
questions.a: Article 89.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers insubordinate conduct toward a warrant officer, NCO, or petty officer?
questions.a: Article 91.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers failure to obey a lawful order or regulation?
questions.a: Article 92.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ is the 'General Article' that covers disorders and neglects prejudicial to good order and discipline?
questions.a: Article 134 — the General Article (also covers conduct of a nature to bring discredit upon the armed forces and crimes not capital).

questions.q: What article covers fraternization?
questions.a: Article 134 (fraternization is a specification under the General Article). It is also addressed in service-specific regulations.

questions.q: What is fraternization under the UCMJ?
questions.a: An improper personal relationship between an officer and an enlisted member that violates the customary bounds of acceptable behavior and is prejudicial to good order and discipline or brings discredit to the armed forces.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers mutiny and sedition?
questions.a: Article 94.

questions.q: What article covers sexual assault under the UCMJ?
questions.a: Article 120 — Rape and Sexual Assault.

questions.q: What article covers murder under the UCMJ?
questions.a: Article 118.

questions.q: What article covers larceny and wrongful appropriation?
questions.a: Article 121.

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ covers malingering (faking illness or injury to avoid duty)?
questions.a: Article 115.

questions.q: What article covers drunken or reckless operation of a vehicle, aircraft, or vessel?
questions.a: Article 113.

questions.q: What is the maximum punishment for being AWOL for more than 30 days?
questions.a: Dishonorable discharge, forfeiture of all pay and allowances, and confinement for 1 year (or 18 months if terminated by apprehension).

questions.q: Can a commander impose punishment under Article 15 for any UCMJ offense?
questions.a: Yes, as long as the commander considers the offense minor. There is no list limiting which offenses can be handled under Article 15.

questions.q: What is the statute of limitations for UCMJ offenses?
questions.a: Generally 5 years from the date of the offense, except for offenses punishable by death (no statute of limitations) and certain sexual offenses (extended or no limit per recent amendments).

questions.q: What article covers contempt toward officials (the President, Vice President, Congress, etc.)?
questions.a: Article 88 (applies to commissioned officers only).

questions.q: What article of the UCMJ addresses unlawful command influence?
questions.a: Article 37 — it prohibits commanders and convening authorities from improperly influencing the actions of courts-martial or the findings/sentencing of court members.

questions.q: Under the UCMJ, can a service member be tried in both a military court-martial and a civilian court for the same act?
questions.a: Yes. The prohibition against double jeopardy applies between courts of the same sovereign. Military courts and state/federal courts are considered separate sovereigns.

questions.q: What is a 'punitive article' of the UCMJ?
questions.a: Articles 77 through 134 (also including newer articles through 134a), which define specific criminal offenses under military law and prescribe maximum punishments.

questions.q: What rights does an accused have at an Article 32 preliminary hearing?
questions.a: Right to be present, right to counsel (military and/or civilian), right to cross-examine witnesses, right to present evidence, and right to a copy of the preliminary hearing officer's report.
